explanation: Procedure sets NOCOUNT OFF rather than ON, leaving row-count messages enabled and adding client/network noise.
XACT_ABORT is OFF while the procedure uses transactions, so some run-time errors can leave work partially applied.
SELECT * couples callers to every current and future column of Sales.Orders.
SELECT TOP (10) on Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the engine returns an arbitrary set of rows.
DISTINCT on o.OrderID is unnecessary because OrderID is the primary key and already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) applies a function to the column and prevents a simple range seek on OrderDate.
Year value 2016 is embedded directly in the predicate instead of being parameterized.
o.CustomerID (int) is compared to @CustomerIdText (nvarchar), forcing an implicit conversion and risking plan/index issues.
OR-based optional filter (@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) hinders cardinality estimation and index seeks.
LIKE N'%urgent%' starts with a wildcard, preventing a normal index seek on Comments.
NOT IN against a subquery on i.CustomerID yields incorrect results if the subquery ever produces NULL.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) wraps the column in a predicate and makes the search non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 performs arithmetic on the column and prevents direct index matching.
Inequality o.CustomerID <> 1 tends to scan broad portions of Sales.Orders.
Business identifier 1 is hard-coded in the CustomerID predicate.
CROSS JOIN of Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies the two sets.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary Orders/Customers pair.
Comma-separated FROM list with join predicate in WHERE is legacy syntax and hides join intent.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary matching OrderID.
Scalar subquery counts OrderLines for every outer Orders row instead of using a set-based join/aggregate.
Correlated EXISTS on OrderLines can be rewritten as a set join for clearer cardinality and plans.
The same CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL THEN 0 ELSE 1 END is repeated in the select list and ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 orders by select-list position, which breaks easily when the select list changes.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate has no stable tie-breaker, so paging can skip or repeat rows.
UNION removes duplicates between branches already disjoint by OrderID = 1 versus OrderID = 2; UNION ALL is sufficient.
OrderID literals 1 and 2 are embedded directly in the query branches.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching OrderLines when only existence is required.
Correlated scalar COUNT subquery runs per outer Orders row to test existence.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID silently keeps the last value if multiple rows are returned.
Table variable @OrderIds can receive poor cardinality estimates when holding multiple rows.
SELECT TOP (5) into @OrderIds has no ORDER BY, so which five OrderIDs are loaded is undefined.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly instead of declaring it explicitly.
SELECT TOP (5) INTO #ImplicitOrders has no ORDER BY, so the loaded rows are arbitrary.
Cursor order_cursor processes rows iteratively when the work can be expressed set-based.
Cursor SELECT TOP (2) has no ORDER BY, so which rows are fetched is undefined.
WHILE loop increments @Counter iteratively instead of using a set-based operation.
Dynamic SQL is built by concatenating @CustomerID into the statement text.
Parameter @CustomerID is concatenated directly into executable SQL, enabling injection if the value is attacker-controlled.
EXEC (@Sql) runs dynamic SQL without parameterized inputs.
Dynamic SQL concatenates @OrderID into the statement instead of binding it as a parameter.
sp_executesql is invoked with a fully inlined string and no parameter definitions/values.
Temporary table #Scratch is created and never dropped before the procedure ends.
CATCH selects ERROR_MESSAGE() but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; THROW is preferred for modern error handling.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics or reliable logging.
CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly instead of using a parameter or lookup.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the OrderDate filter.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') omits a style parameter, so day/month order depends on session settings.
COALESCE on both sides of the Orders/Invoices join predicate inhibits normal join optimization.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary joined row.
Self-join of Sales.Orders on OrderID only retrieves the same key already available on the outer row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary Orders row from the self-join.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts on an unselective nvarchar(MAX) text/LOB column.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact table without a restrictive filter.
Row-level predicate ol.OrderID > 0 is applied in HAVING instead of WHERE.
ORDER BY on COUNT(*) without GROUP BY is unnecessary because the aggregate returns one row.
MERGE is used despite documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; prefer separate INSERT/UPDATE.
MERGE UPDATE sets target.Comments = target.Comments, producing writes/logging with no data change.
UPDATE sets Comments = Comments, causing locking and logging without changing data.
DELETE from Sales.OrderLines uses broad non-key predicate Quantity = -1 rather than a stable key.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is started outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON couples the procedure to fragile identity-management session state.
EXEC sp_who invokes a system routine without a schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes session behavior and is never restored to 0.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits an explicit column list.
CategoryID value 99 is hard-coded in the INSERT.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' uses a leading wildcard and prevents a normal seek on CustomerName.
Dynamic SQL concatenates @CustomerName into the LIKE pattern string.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into executable SQL before sp_executesql.
Dynamic LIKE pattern built with a leading % prevents seeks on CustomerName.
sp_executesql runs @sql without parameter bindings; the value is already inlined.
EXEC sp_executesql is called without a schema qualifier (e.g., sys.sp_executesql).
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and will modify every row in the temp table.
CustomerCategoryID is set to hard-coded literal 1.
Predicate on outer-joined o.OrderDate in WHERE eliminates NULL-extended rows and turns the LEFT JOIN into an inner join.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded in the OrderDate filter.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL uses equality to NULL; it should be IS NULL or the predicate never matches.
Scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) in the SELECT list forces row-by-row evaluation.
CATCH after BEGIN TRANSACTION does not roll back before handling the error.
CATCH only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows nor signals failure to the caller.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes an index hint and couples the query to a physical index id.
Three-plus levels of nested subqueries (t1/t2/t3) should be refactored to CTEs or temp tables.
LIKE '%A%' starts with a wildcard and prevents a normal index seek on CustomerName.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti returns every column and couples callers to the function's shape.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF used where an inline TVF would optimize better.
